input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>

+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>

+</Project>

data.content: Need to configure BenchmarkDotNet to handle multi-targeting properly.
data.reasoningText: BenchmarkDotNet handles multi-targeting itself - I need to run from one framework but configure it to benchmark both. Let me adjust the approach - I'll use BenchmarkDotNet's `[SimpleJob]` attribute with different runtimes.

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-9d4aceb13f7149aa9756b18216d98608/Benchmarks && dotnet run -c Release --framework net10.0 -- --filter '*' --join 2>&1", "description": "Run benchmarks for .NET 9 and 10", "initial_wait": 300, "mode": "sync"}

data.content:
Excellent results! Here's the comparison:

## .NET 9 → .NET 10 Benchmark Results

| Method | .NET 9 (Mean) | .NET 10 (Mean) | Speedup | Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AverageWordLength** | 24.54 μs | 1.41 μs | **~17x faster** | 0 B (same) |
| **WordLengthHistogram** | 44.95 μs | 4.01 μs | **~11x faster** | 112 B (same) |
| **UniqueWordCount** | 67.96 μs | 26.29 μs | **~2.6x faster** | 1552 B (same) |

All three methods show significant performance improvements on .NET 10. Memory allocations remain unchanged. The large gains in `AverageWordLength` and `WordLengthHistogram` likely come from improved `ReadOnlyCollection<int>` indexer devirtualization/inlining in .NET 10's JIT. `UniqueWordCount` also benefits, though more modestly.

**Caveat:** The .NET 9 run showed high StdDev (the machine may have been under load), so the exact speedup ratios should be taken with a grain of salt — but the directional improvement is clear.
